⬚ 20m Grammar/Comp: Grade 9 - Lesson 1
Correctness
⍞ Materials: Essay Voyage

➜ INTRO
This book will help you grow your writing skills in all areas. It is especially aimed at helping you learn how to write formal academic essays.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Essay Voyage p.1-4

➜ PRACTICE
1\. Why is the grammar of vocabulary words important to think about?
2\. Write a short sentence using superfluous as an adjective and another using superfluity as a noun.
3\. Write a short sentence using adhere or adhered as a verb and another using adherent as a noun or adhering as an adjective.
4\. Write two sentences that use at least one form of each word. For example, I have never been an adherent of expending superfluous energy.

★ STUDENT NOTE
In this book, the word essay refers to a formal academic essay.

In your other composition lessons, we use the term essay to refer to a short piece of writing on a particular topic. This is the primary dictionary definition of the word. Different types of essays in different settings require different ways of writing.

Hopefully, this will prevent any confusion about the use of the word essay in your lessons.

⬚ 20m Grammar/Comp: Grade 9 - Lesson 2
Correctness
⍞ Materials: Essay Voyage

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Essay Voyage p.5

➜ PRACTICE
Complete the four-level analysis on p.6. Remember to come up with some interesting points about the sentence in the comment section. The comments in the Answer Key are only a guide or possibilities of what you might have noticed.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
Essay Voyage p.7

➜ PRACTICE
What do the comma rules do for a sentence? Complete the exercise on p.8 and check your answers.

★ STUDENT NOTE
∞ PDF Link: Essay Voyage Answer Key

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 1
Asking Questions

➜ INTRO
As in nature study where we ask ourselves questions to help us observe, so too when we read, we want to ask questions of ourselves to help us observe what is in the text. As we practice noticing and wondering about our books, we will see more to ponder and enjoy.

➜ WRITE
Ask yourself questions about one of your readings and write your answers. Here are a few questions to get you started:

• What am I noticing?
• What am I wondering about?
• What does this remind me of?
• What are my initial reactions?
• Why does the author say this?
• How does this relate to other things I have read?
• Why do I think the author wrote in this way?

★ STUDENT/TEACHER NOTE
In addition to these more formal composition lessons, be sure to keep up the habit of written and oral narrations throughout the week. Aim for 8-10 written narrations per week, and use different ways of narrating to keep it interesting!

You may write or type your narrations and compositions initially. But for these composition lessons, be sure to type the final draft that you turn in to your teacher.

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 2
Tech/Visual

➜ DESIGN
Over the year, you'll create your own reference book for the various poetic forms you study in Poetry Study: Grades 9-12. The purpose is for you to have a quick guide that makes sense to you and is easy to pull up or keep on hand as you read individual poems. Start by creating your own "At a Glance" for the Villanelle. Here are a few of the possibilities:

• Copy the "Villanelle At a Glance" into a designated space in a notebook or a document on a device where you can easily find and reference it in the future. Add any additional notes for yourself that you'd like.
• Draw an illustration or create an infographic in Canva (or similar design software) showing the form more visually.
• Make a PowerPoint and create your own Villanelle at a glance for the first slide (using text and/or visuals).
• Something else! Get creative!

• REFERENCE
Poetry Study: Grades 9-12 (Lesson 1)
Book: Making of a Poem

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 7
Research/Essay

➜ WRITE
Pick a controversial event, law, or situation you've read about recently in Current Events and research it. Summarize the perspective of each side in your own words. Try to write in such a way that each side would agree with how you have described their perspective. Why do people believe the way they do on both sides? You will have another lesson to add your thoughts on which perspective is best. Today, focus on understanding the different perspectives and some of the reasons for them and then articulating them in your own words.

• REFERENCE
Current Events
Resource: Variety of News Sites

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 11
Senses

➜ INTRO
Writers add many details about what their characters are sensing to help us enter into the story. Good descriptions immerse us in the sights, sounds, feel, tastes, and smells of a situation.

➜ WRITE
Take 15 minutes and think back over yesterday or your morning and write down descriptive words and phrases for what you have seen, tasted, heard, felt, and smelled. For example, think about the color of the light in your room or outside, the texture and taste of your food, the feel of your chair, bed, or couch, your feet on the floor, the temperature, or your pencil, pen, or keyboard, the colors and shapes around you, and the smells inside and out.

Then take your descriptive words and phrases and write a full paragraph or two describing your day or morning.

★ STUDENT TIP
As you read this week, pay attention to the sensory details authors include. What kinds of details can you find in your books? Mostly sight descriptions? Smells, sounds, tastes?

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 12
Editing

➜ REVISE
Pull up your Current Events assignment from Lessons 7 and 10. You'll spend increasingly more time on the editing and revision stages of writing over the next several years. Today, work through the following steps to edit your work:

1\. Read it aloud.
2\. Add additional thoughts and ideas to your paper.
3\. Fix any grammar or spelling errors you hear and see.
4\. Use Grammarly or another spell checker to fix any additional errors.
5\. Are your paragraphs indented?
6\. Is each paragraph focused on one idea?

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 17
Plot

➜ INTRO
How do authors keep you interested in a story or movie? Name as many ways as you can think of.

➜ REFLECT
Reread the opening paragraphs of Ch.10 in The Scarlet Letter. What development in Chillingworth's character is described in the first paragraph? How does Nathaniel Hawthorne use this new information to move the story along? What questions or suspicions does he raise in you, the reader, with this intro?

Now look at the concluding paragraphs of Ch.10. How does Hawthorne build the suspense of the novel with the conclusion of the chapter as well?

➜ READ
Read this article on suspense and look for ways authors hold your interest and move their story forward as you continue reading The Scarlet Letter and your other books!
∞ Article Link: Building Suspense

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 19
Dialogue

➜ INTRO
Have you ever thought about the differences between how we speak and how we write? Flip through your church history book, "Renewal", and notice when people are speaking. Notice the paragraph indents, punctuation, and the way the author introduces or guides you through the conversation. As one author suggests, when writing dialogue, "Keep your words and sentences short. Break up the speeches with the other person’s questions, gestures, or thoughts. Start a new paragraph whenever the next person speaks, does something, or thinks. Short bursts of speech seem natural to the reader. Just as long speech paragraphs seem unnatural, so do long speech sentences. and so do long words" (from Writing Magic by Gail Levine).

➜ WRITE
Make up two characters (or pick two characters from the same or different books) and write a conversation including dialogue between the two of them. Here are some options:
• Have several of the Greek scientists discuss some of their new ideas with each other.
• Have Judge Dee talk to Hester.
• Write a story with dialogue of someone who found themselves in trouble due to their habit of exaggeration.
• Rewrite Adam & Eve's conversation in Book IX of Paradise Lost in modern-day English.

Save a few minutes at the end of the lesson and read your composition aloud. Does the dialogue sound natural?

• REFERENCE
Church History: Grades 7-9
Book: Renewal

Examples given from Ancient History, General Literature, Poetry Reading (Lesson 20), and Ethics: Part 3 (Lesson 10).

OR
Whose view of sin and repentance is right, Hester's or Dimmsdale's? (First, describe each of their perspectives on sin and repentance.)

• REFERENCE

➜ REVISE

1\. If you wrote it by hand, make sure and type it up now.
2\. Read it aloud.
3\. Add additional thoughts and ideas to your paper.
4\. Fix any grammar or spelling errors you hear and see.
5\. Use Grammarly or another spell checker to fix any additional errors.
6\. Are your paragraphs indented?
7\. Is each paragraph focused on one idea?

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 26
Beginnings

➜ INTRO
Authors use a variety of ways to get you interested in their book. They might start in the middle of the action, draw you in with a question, immerse you in the setting, introduce you to a character, or something else.

➜ ACTIVITY
Grab 5 of your favorite books off the shelf. Read the first paragraph or page of each. How do the authors start each book? What makes you want to keep reading?

➜ WRITE
Think of an exciting experience in your own life. Make a list of several possible beginnings for how you could tell the story. What kinds of beginnings capture your attention?

★ STUDENT TIP
As you begin new books for school or on your own time, notice how they begin.

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 27
Questions/ Essay topics

➜ REFLECT
Pick a book or topic you've read about recently. Write 5 questions or topics related to your readings that have piqued your curiosity. Feel free to look back at your book while you think and write. One side effect of this type of reflective thinking is that it is an effective way to generate ideas for essays or other writing assignments.

Here are some of the questions you could ask yourself:

• What am I noticing?
• What sensory details does the author include?
• How is the author building the narrative and keeping my attention?
• What am I wondering about?
• What does this remind me of?
• What are my initial reactions?
• Why does the author say \_\_\_?
• How does \_\_\_ relate to other things I have read?
• Why did the author choose to write from this point of view? How would the book be different if it was written from a different POV?
• What do I want to know more about?

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 28
Beginnings Practice

➜ WRITE
You will experiment more with different beginnings today. Choose a story from your own life or something you've read to write or rewrite, and compose at least three different beginnings. You don't have to write the whole story. Focus on the beginning of each.

For example, think about The Story of the Three Little Pigs. Instead of starting when the pigs set off to build their houses, what if you started the story when the pig in the stick house had to flee or when the wolf is coming down the chimney into the brick or stone house? How would the altered beginning affect the story?

★ STUDENT TIP
If you get stuck choosing a story, just use the story of the Three Little Pigs or another similar tale!

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 29
Identifying & Imitating Style

➜ INTRO
Have you ever seen or done an impression of someone else? It's often funny to watch people's impressions of famous actors, politicians, or family members because we all have mannerisms which are unique to us and/or our families. We might talk with our hands, overuse a particular exclamation, or consistently have a certain tone (excited, gloomy, etc.!). Have you ever thought about the fact that authors have mannerisms, too? The mannerism or "style" of an author is often something you unconsciously pick up on but is also something you can consciously notice or consider. Some noticeable aspects of an author's tone and style are due to their historical time period, and some are their own personal flair. Think about Shakespeare's style of writing. If someone read you some lines from a play you hadn't read yet, you might still be able to guess it was written by Shakespeare because you recognized his style.

An author's style, like personal mannerisms, is created by many things such as an author's choice of words, sentence structure and length, the amount and type of descriptive words they use, or even the tone of their work. Their tone or "voice" might be cheerful and carefree, ominous and sad, formal, chatty, excited, etc.

➜ WRITE
For the rest of this lesson, pretend you are one of your favorite authors and try writing in that author's style. Write a paragraph or two about something you are reading, something in your day, or make something up. If you find it difficult to pick an author to imitate, use Shakespeare or try writing as if you were someone else you know in real life (maybe your best friend, a parent, a public speaker, etc.).

Read your work aloud to someone (who would be familiar with the author you're imitating), and see if they can guess who you're trying to imitate.

★ STUDENT TIP
As you read throughout the week, try to notice authors' style, and feel free to incorporate anything you notice into your regular narrations (oral or written). What is it about an author's writing that lets you know they are from a particular time, place, or culture? Do they repeat certain words or types of words often? How would you describe their tone?

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 30
Showing & Telling

➜ INTRO
Describe the difference between these two sentences:

Sentence 1: "Bobby was excited!"
Sentence 2: "Bobby smiled widely, clapped his hands, and let out a joyful whoop!"

Both sentences help you understand that Bobby is excited about something. The first sentence simply tells you this directly. The second shows you Bobby's excitement by giving you details to vividly see Bobby's excitement in your mind's eye.

Authors use descriptive language and details to show what is happening and invite readers to imagine the setting, characters, and action in a story. When an author does a lot of "showing," the action slows down, there may be dialogue, and the characters' thoughts, feelings, and sensations are clearly described. When an author mostly "tells," a lot may happen in only a few paragraphs. Authors use a combination of showing and telling to keep the story moving and to keep your interest. If authors only focus on "telling," you might not get interested in a character or be able to visualize the action of a story. If the book is all "showing," you might lose interest because the pace is so slow.

➜ INSPECT
Gather a few literature books off your shelves and inspect a few pages of each to see if you can find examples of telling and showing.

➜ WRITE
Write a scene based on your own experience (riding your bike, making a cake, raking leaves, etc.), something you’re reading, or something you make up. Think about the details you’re including and try to “show” rather than “tell.” Try to write a whole page before you finish writing about all the details in your scene. Have fun!

★ STUDENT TIP
Keep an eye out for examples of showing and telling in your readings!

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 43
Editing Prose

➜ REVISE
Choose either your assignment from Lesson 31, 33, or 35 to edit today. Writers often let their work sit for a bit after initially writing their thoughts because it's easier to look at your writing as a reader would when the ideas are not as fresh in your head.

1\. Make sure you are working in a typed version of your assignment.
2\. Read it aloud.
3\. Add additional thoughts and ideas to your paper.
4\. Fix any grammar or spelling errors you hear and see.
5\. Use Grammarly or another spell checker to fix any additional errors.
6\. Are your paragraphs indented?
7\. Is each paragraph focused on one idea?
8\. Give your work a title!

Share your revised narration with someone.

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 44
Editing Poetry

➜ REVISE
Pick a poem you've written this term (or you can pick one from last term) to revise. Revising poetry requires a different process than editing other types of writing. There are so few words in a poem (compared to a whole book or essay) that it makes each word even more important.

1\. Type your poem if you haven't already.
2\. Revisit the rules of your poem's form (sonnet, sestina, ballad, etc.)
3\. Read it aloud.
4\. Does your poem follow the rules of its form? (Rhyme, meter, line count, etc.) If you are breaking any of the rules, decide if you are breaking them for a reason, to make a point, or if you need to find a different word or phrase to fit the rhyme scheme or meter. (Note: if you choose to break a rule, add a note about why this is important for your poem.)
5\. Think about the flow of the ideas and images in your poem. Is there a better way to organize them?
6\. Imagery and metaphor are important in nearly all poems. What images and comparisons does your poem use, and are there any others that might capture the image or idea even better?
7\. Read your poem aloud again and look for any grammar or spelling errors to fix. Use Grammarly or another spell checker to assist you.
8\. Jot down a few of the things you want your reader to walk away thinking or feeling after hearing your poem. Go read your poem aloud to someone (or two!) and ask them what their initial impressions are. If they are completely different than what you intended, consider what you might want to alter so that your purpose comes through more clearly.

★ STUDENT TIP
If you aren't comfortable reading your poem aloud to someone, consider just giving them a printed version of your poem. Let them read it and then either tell you or give you a few impressions they've jotted down.

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 59
Edit/Revising Narrations

➜ REVISE
Choose a narration or a composition assignment (at least two paragraphs long) to edit and revise today.

1\. Make sure you are working in a typed version of your work.
2\. Read it aloud.
3\. Add additional thoughts and ideas. Ask yourself what a reader who was only somewhat familiar with your topic might want to know in addition to what you have already written.
4\. Fix any grammar or spelling errors you hear and see.
5\. Use Grammarly or another spell checker to fix any additional errors.
6\. Are your paragraphs indented?
7\. Is each paragraph focused on one idea? One way to figure it out is to summarize your paragraph in one sentence.
8\. Read your work aloud again and think about your tone. For this assignment, try to make your tone friendly and formal.

Share your revised narration with someone.

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 60
Editing a Scene

➜ REVISE
Revise your scenes from Lesson 51.

1\. Spend a few minutes adding any additional details, dialogue, or scenes to your play.
2\. Read your play aloud to check punctuation, grammar, and spelling.
3\. Use Grammarly or another spell checker to fix any additional errors.
4\. Read your dialogue aloud and revise. Does it sound natural? (We tend to speak in short sentences and sentence fragments.)
5\. Add instructions for props or setting as desired.
6\. Be sure to include the setting for each scene (i.e., the front yard of a house, a boat, etc.).
7\. Make a plan to have some friends perform your play!

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 65
Essay - Understanding Perspectives

➜ WRITE
Current Event: Pick a topic and research. Summarize the perspective of each side in your own words, including brief quotes from each perspective to support your summary. Hyperlink any articles you reference, as in a news article. Aim to give the best points from each perspective, and imagine talking to a person who believed in each. Would they agree that you understand their perspective and have summarized it well?

Here is a sample outline of how your essay might end up, though you are free to use a different structure as long as you include each of the components. Work on the middle two paragraphs today. You will have three lesson periods to work on this essay.

\- 1 short paragraph introducing the topic you're going to talk about and why you think it's important
\- 1 paragraph explaining one point of view and the reasons for it
\- 1 paragraph explaining the opposite point of view and the reasons
\- 1 paragraph explaining which perspective you find most convincing and why

• REFERENCE
Current Events

★ STUDENT/TEACHER TIP
The purpose of this essay is to work on researching the conversation about a particular topic, articulating both sides well in your own words, and giving your thoughts. Try to choose a topic that interests you and will be relatively simple to research. For instance, a topic like whether a particular town should add a train line is a better topic for this assignment than something large and complicated to research, such as whether to vote republican, democrat, or third party.

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 67
Editing & Revision: Current Events Project

➜ REVISE
Big-Picture Revision
1\. Are there other ideas you've had more recently that you'd like to add to your current events essay? Do that now.
2\. Look at your paragraphs. Are they indented and focused around one idea each? Describe that main idea in one sentence and consider making that the first sentence (i.e., topic sentence) of your paragraph.

Sentence-Level Revision
1\. Did you link to the articles you drew from to learn about different perspectives?
2\. Read your paper aloud and fix any grammar errors you hear and see.
3\. Use Grammarly or another spell checker to find any other punctuation or spelling issues and fix those.
4\. Give your essay a title. (Think of it like an article about a news event!)
5\. Look for wordiness. Are there any unnecessary words? Do you have any sentences that are very long that you could make into two shorter sentences? Long sentences have their place, but usually, shorter sentences are clearer.
6\. Make sure your sentences are complete, not fragments.
7\. Make sure your essay is consistently in 3rd person while reporting others' perspectives. If desired, you may switch to 1st person when talking about your opinions in the final paragraph(s).

⬚ 30m Composition: Grade 9 - Lesson 68
Revision - 1st Person POV Description

➜ REVISE
Today, you'll revise your diary entry from Lesson 64.

Big-Picture Revision
1\. Read your diary entry aloud.
2\. Where did you begin the story? Brainstorm at least two other places you could have Mr. Darcy or Mr. Collins begin the story. Choose the one you like best and rework the beginning if necessary.
3\. Once you've revised your beginning, consider the flow of the rest of your narrative. Is there anything you'd like to change?
4\. Read over your writing again and consider the details you include. Remember how "telling" moves the action forward and "showing" moves slower but helps us experience the story. Make sure you are both showing and telling throughout your writing. (Pay attention to the sensory details you include as well!).
5\. Consider the tone of your writing. Is it the tone you want?
6\. Look for wordiness and delete unnecessary words.
7\. Read your diary entry one more time and look for grammar or spelling errors to fix. Use Grammarly or another spell checker to assist you as well.

Turn in your revised description!

★ STUDENT/TEACHER TIP
Since the whole diary entry is in a 1st person perspective, the difference between showing or telling is also coming through the eyes of Mr. Darcy or Mr. Collins. Here is an example of the difference between the two from a 1st person perspective:

Telling: "I wept."
Showing: "I felt moisture on my face and salt on my tongue."

• EXTRA HELPINGS
Consider adding illustrations to your work!
